topic: Cuban coffee -- best colada in Miami debate

turns.content: I need to settle something. My husband and I have been arguing about this for literally decades. Where in Miami do you get the best colada?

Ha, that's a debate that could start a civil war in Miami! It really depends on what you value most in a colada. A few of the top contenders:

- **Versailles** on Calle Ocho -- the classic, the institution. Consistent, strong, and the ventanita experience is iconic.
- **La Carreta** -- similar old-guard Cuban coffee, some people prefer it slightly sweeter.
- **Exquisito Restaurant** -- a sleeper pick on Flagler. Their colada is very strong, less sweet, more traditional.
- **Cafe La Trova** -- if you want a more modern, upscale take.

What's your side of the argument and what's his?

See, I am a Versailles loyalist. I have been going to that ventanita since before it was a tourist attraction. My husband swears by this little place on Bird Road -- El Palacio de los Jugos. He says their colada is stronger. I say stronger is not the same as better.

You make an excellent legal distinction there -- stronger is absolutely not the same as better. A colada should be balanced: strong enough to wake the dead, sweet enough to make you smile, and smooth enough that it doesn't feel like you're drinking battery acid.

El Palacio de los Jugos does make a very potent cup, but their colada can be inconsistent -- sometimes it's perfect, sometimes it's basically espresso with sugar thrown at it. Versailles has the edge on consistency. You go to that ventanita at 7 AM or 3 PM and you're getting the same quality.

That said, your husband isn't wrong that El Palacio has a certain raw power to it. Have you tried doing a blind taste test? That might settle it once and for all.
A blind taste test. You know, I am a judge. I should have thought of that. The evidence would speak for itself. But knowing him, he would claim I rigged the proceedings.

Ha! Sounds like you need an impartial jury. Get a few friends or neighbors to be the panel -- four or five people who don't know which cup is which. Number the cups, present them blind, tally the votes. Transparent process, no grounds for appeal.

You could even make it a little event -- buy a colada from Versailles, one from El Palacio, and maybe throw in a wild card from a third spot just to keep it interesting. Majority rules. And if he still claims you rigged it, well... that's what appellate courts are for.
I like the wild card idea. Maybe Exquisito -- you mentioned them. I have actually never tried theirs. What is their style? Is it very sweet or more on the bitter side?

Exquisito leans more traditional -- less sweet than Versailles, with a more pronounced bitterness and a thicker crema on top. The espresso itself is darker roasted, so you get more of those smoky, almost chocolatey notes. It's the kind of colada that old-timers tend to prefer because it tastes like what their abuelas used to make.

If your husband likes the raw strength of El Palacio, he might actually gravitate toward Exquisito in a blind test. So be warned -- the wild card could end up being his ally, not yours.

For what it's worth, the crema is really where you can judge quality. A good colada should have that light tan foam on top -- espumita -- that's sweet and almost caramelized. If the espumita is thin or nonexistent, the coffee was rushed.

Espumita. Yes. That is exactly what separates a real colada from someone just pouring cafecito into a styrofoam cup and calling it a day. You cannot rush the espumita. My mother used to make it at home and she would stand there with the first few drops of espresso and sugar, beating it with a spoon until it was perfect. It was a ritual.

Your mother knew the secret. That technique -- catching the first drops and whipping them with the sugar -- is everything. It emulsifies the sugar into the coffee oils and creates that real, proper espumita that holds its structure. Most shops use machines now, but the ones that still do it by hand, or at least approximate that method, produce a noticeably better cup.

It really is a ritual, you're right. There's something about the patience it requires that makes the coffee taste better. You can't microwave your way to a good colada.

Sounds like you already know exactly what makes a great one. Versailles may be your spot for buying it, but your mother's kitchen was probably the real gold standard.

It was. Ay, now I am getting nostalgic. She would make colada every afternoon at three o'clock without fail. The whole house smelled like it. And she would pour a little cup for everyone -- neighbors, whoever happened to be there. That is the thing about colada that people from outside Miami do not understand. It is not just coffee. It is social.

You've hit on something essential. The colada is designed to be shared -- that's why it comes in that larger cup with the little plastic thimbles on the side. It's a communal act. Your mother pouring cups for the neighbors at three o'clock is the whole tradition in miniature.

That social dimension is also why the ventanita matters so much. Standing outside Versailles with your colada, talking to whoever is next to you -- that's not just buying coffee, that's participating in a cultural ritual. People who order Cuban coffee through a drive-through are technically getting the same product but missing the entire point.

Exactly. You cannot separate the coffee from the context. That is what I will tell my husband when I present the results of the taste test -- the setting matters. Versailles has the ventanita. El Palacio is a fruit stand with a coffee window. The atmosphere is part of the evidence.

Now you're entering closing arguments territory and I think the jury would be persuaded. Though a defense attorney might object that atmosphere is prejudicial rather than probative -- the taste test should be blind, after all.

But off the record? You're right. Coffee is never just about what's in the cup. It's about where you are when you drink it and who you're with. That's a verdict the evidence fully supports.

You are absolutely right about that. Nobody has ever beaten my mother's colada, and nobody ever will. Claro, I am biased. But the evidence supports my position. Thank you for this -- I am going to organize this taste test. My husband will lose, and I will be gracious about it.

I love the confidence. Gracious in victory is the mark of a true professional. Good luck with the taste test -- and if by some chance he wins, I trust you'll handle defeat with equal dignity.

Enjoy the coladas, and give your mother's recipe its proper place at the top of the rankings, even if it's not in the competition. Some things are beyond judgment.
